as the two big holdouts of directors who began their careers in the new hollywood era the filmography of scorsese and spielberg always fascinated me especially the contrast between both of their careers both of them were snubbed for best director for their breakout films jaws and taxi driver even though the movie was nominated for best picture in favour of acclaimed foreign directors fellini and bergman whose movies werent nominated for best picture both of them suffered their biggest setback in the late 1970s with 1941 and new york new york and made an immediate comeback with their next films in the early 80s which are considered to be one of the best of their career raiders of the lost ark raging bull this is in contrast to someone like coppola or friedkin who were sunk quite badly for a long time by one from the heart or sorcerer in the 80s there is a interesting contrast between the two directors where spielberg is the king of box office yet critics refused to see his movies as prestigious or adult while scorsese movies are considered to be prestigious or adult but arent earning any money at the box office it took till the 90s where both directors were able overcome this image scorsese movies like cape fear and goodfellas start earning decent money for their budget while spielberg made schindlers list which immediately shut everyone up about his movies not being prestigious or adult its even more interesting considering that scorsese and spielberg had actually traded cape fear and schindlers list which in a strange way allowed them to go to the opposite side of what they usually do the most nominated editors for oscars along with the ones who have won the most oscars for editing are micheal kahn and thelma schoonmaker both of whom have won 3 oscars each for movies directed by spielberg and scorsese respectively both of them have been nominated 8 times out of which 7 nominations are for movies directed by spielberg and scorsese respectively scorsese is the 2nd most nominated director for best director oscar while spielberg is the 3rd most nominated director for best director oscar even if spielberg is nominated again for west side story for best director he will still be the 3rd most nominated director after scorsese both of them have recently gotten controversy over their statements over recent trends in movies with spielberg it was regarding his comments about netflix movies should not be nominated for oscars while scorsese got controversy for his comments on the mcu films my roommate and i got into a pretty intense discussion on which spielberg film is better jurassic park or minority report even though both of these live within the scifi realm i know and understand they are vastly different films meant for different demographics nevertheless i firmly believe that as a stand alone film without taking into consideration their cultural impact minority report is a superior film that is not to say jurassic park isnt a great movie its a beautiful and joyful experience one with many memorable moments that went down in film history minority report in my opinion is just so much more interesting in terms of plot concept execution acting and writing an extremely captivating film just from the story alone and utterly more sophisticated than many of spielbergs great films i dont try to be one of those jaded film school kids that suck on jurassic park and jaws just because they are natural classics maybe personally im just much more interested in films that are more obscure complex and provocative rather than a feel in recent memory when analyzing the films power and what makes it work so well most folks often fall back to the few common concepts that inevitably arise whenever any wes anderson film is discussed the quirky screenplay the incredible production design the trademark cinematography and of course bill murray but one of the more glossed over parts of what anderson does so well in his films is the ability to get really interesting and remarkably revelatory performances out of actors weve known for ages from the aforementioned bill murray to bruce willis anderson is able to cut deep into what an actor really brings to the table and expose it in an interesting often drastically different manner nowhere is this more readily apparent than in what he and the perennially underrated ralph fiennes created with monsieur gustav h in the grand budapest hotel before i continue ask yourselves for a moment what do you typically think of when mr fiennes is mentioned a menacing amon goeth from schindlers list perhaps a domineering m from the james bond franchise or how about a campy voldemort from harry potter these are all memorable performances from the dapper englishman but they also all feel like they share a certain common quality right of course they are all antagonistic and in some cases outright villainous roles in nature but theres some deeper connection that fiennes naturally brings to me it can be best summed up in this incredible piercing glare he gives with each performance he often weaponizes his eyes in such a way to see through any facade or at the very least instill a great deal of unease this intense perceptiveness is fienness greatest tool as an actor and given how well he wields it its no wonder that hes mostly typecast in villainous roles after all whats more terrifying than a foe that can see right through you and instill fear from just a glance but whats fascinating about the grand budapest hotel is that the film uses this same perceptive quality from fiennes to deliver an acting transformation that weve truly never seen from him or really anyone for that matter before fienness monsieur gustav h is the suave fastidious concierge of the titular hotel which likely puts in good company to be considered one of the greatest concierges in all of europe at first gustav feels very much in line with other traditional fiennesian performances in his introduction to zero and by extension our introduction to him he tears down the poor emigres experience and aptitude for the job in a brutal and unrelenting manner during this scene one immediately notices that the same eyes used to terrorize harry potter or intimidate james bond are also used in a similar fashion here to test the hapless lobby boy but as harsh as gustav is in maintaining the prestige and excellence of the hotel in most other respects he is nothing more than a delightful man childlike in his celebration of others in quiet moments of intimacy fienness same trademark intense glares morph into wideeyed curious stares the same intense perceptive quality is present with this shift but what changes is its effect if a rich dowager in tilda swintons madame d is ghoulish and decaying on the outside gustav finds a wounded sensitive woman whos wealth has alienated herself from others and left her in a skittish condition fiennes wields his eyes in scenes between them with concentrated fervor equal to that of his previous more menacing performances but the quality of this intensity is that of a benevolent demeanor rather than of the destructive one hes typically ascribed to with such powerfully warm stares one finds that gustav goes the distance to sympathize with madame ds good traits rather than easily deconstruct her faults if a prison mate is brutish and vulgar in his physical appearance gustave is able to look past his rough scarred exterior in favor of something more delicate hidden inside the simple act of viewing him as another person on his level while offering middling prison food earns him a delicate level of compassion and loyalty an act of simple humanity which pays off shortly thereafter as gustav makes his escape and i think this encapsulates what makes it such a great performance to me where previous performances from fiennes relied on his ability to use his intense perception to intimidate and identify weaknesses in his foes to achieve his goals budapest flips that formula on its head requiring fiennes to use that same ability to instead absorb and draw out the best qualities in even the worst people its one of the finest examples in recent memory of a director identifying a powerful trait in an actor and applying it in a dramatically different context to tremendous success a truly wonderful and substantive memory when analyzing the films power and what makes it work so well most folks often fall back to the few common concepts that inevitably arise whenever any wes anderson film is discussed the quirky screenplay the incredible production design the trademark cinematography and of course bill murray but one of the more glossed over parts of what anderson does so well in his films is the ability to get really interesting and remarkably revelatory performances out of actors weve known for ages from the aforementioned bill murray to bruce willis anderson is able to cut deep into what an actor really brings to the table and expose it in an interesting often drastically different manner nowhere is this more readily apparent than in what he and the perennially underrated ralph fiennes created with monsieur gustav h in the grand budapest hotel before i continue ask yourselves for a moment what do you typically think of when mr fiennes is mentioned a menacing amon goeth from schindlers list perhaps a domineering m from the james bond franchise or how about a campy voldemort from harry potter these are all memorable performances from the dapper englishman but they also all feel like they share a certain common quality right of course they are all antagonistic and in some cases outright villainous roles in nature but theres some deeper connection that fiennes naturally brings to me it can be best summed up in this incredible piercing glare he gives with each performance he often weaponizes his eyes in such a way to see through any facade or at the very least instill a great deal of unease this intense perceptiveness is fienness greatest tool as an actor and given how well he wields it its no wonder that hes mostly typecast in villainous roles after all whats more terrifying than a foe that can see right through you and instill fear from just a glance but whats fascinating about the grand budapest hotel is that the film uses this same perceptive quality from fiennes to deliver an acting transformation that weve truly never seen from him or really anyone for that matter before fienness monsieur gustav h is the suave fastidious concierge of the titular hotel which likely puts in good company to be considered one of the greatest concierges in all of europe at first gustav feels very much in line with other traditional fiennesian performances in his introduction to zero and by extension our introduction to him he tears down the poor emigres experience and aptitude for the job in a brutal and unrelenting manner during this scene one immediately notices that the same eyes used to terrorize harry potter or intimidate james bond are also used in a similar fashion here to test the hapless lobby boy but as harsh as gustav is in maintaining the prestige and excellence of the hotel in most other respects he is nothing more than a delightful man childlike in his celebration of others in quiet moments of intimacy fienness same trademark intense glares morph into wideeyed curious stares the same intense perceptive quality is present with this shift but what changes is its effect if a rich dowager in tilda swintons madame d is ghoulish and decaying on the outside gustav finds a wounded sensitive woman whos wealth has alienated herself from others and left her in a skittish condition fiennes wields his eyes in scenes between them with concentrated fervor equal to that of his previous more menacing performances but the quality of this intensity is that of a benevolent demeanor rather than of the destructive one hes typically ascribed to with such powerfully warm stares one finds that gustav goes the distance to sympathize with madame ds good traits rather than easily deconstruct her faults if a prison mate is brutish and vulgar in his physical appearance gustave is able to look past his rough scarred exterior in favor of something more delicate hidden inside the simple act of viewing him as another person on his level while offering middling prison food earns him a delicate level of compassion and loyalty an act of simple humanity which pays off shortly thereafter as gustav makes his escape and i think this encapsulates what makes it such a great performance to me where previous performances from fiennes relied on his ability to use his intense perception to intimidate and identify weaknesses in his foes to achieve his goals budapest flips that formula on its head requiring fiennes to use that same ability to instead absorb and draw out the best qualities in even the worst people its one of the finest examples in recent memory of a director identifying a powerful trait in an actor and applying it in a dramatically different context to tremendous success a truly wonderful and substantive transformation in performance a couple for those who are unfamiliar with the above phrase in political terminologies only nixon can go to china refers to an instance where a politician with a well known reputation of upholding certain political positions can have the credibility to take actions that are in defiance of those positions and not be destroyed politically richard nixon who was an anti communist his whole life was the only one who could soften relations with communist china in 1972 without his credibility being destroyed what are some instances of that for filmmakers actors in regards to creating or starting in a who has already created a seminal work in regards to jewish suffering schindlers list could have possibly made a film about the cycle of violence between the israelis and palestinians that condemns both sides in 2005 and not have his reputation hurt the quote goes something like this frederic raphael tells stanley kubrick that schindlers list was a good representation of the holocaust kubrick replies think thats about the holocaust that was about success wasnt it the holocaust is about 6 million people who get killed schindlers list is about 600 who dont a couple of things to note the person who reported that quote is the aforementioned frederic raphael screenwriter of eyes wide shut and who himself admitted to having an acrimonious relationship with kubrick in his memoir eyes wide open where the quote originates the book has been criticized by everyone around kubrick family friends and cohorts as misleading and unfair so i dont think that quote can be taken at face value not to mention kubrick and spielberg were close friends and discussed their lives both personal and professional all the time including schindlers list the two would famously collaborate on ai had marathon long phone conversations talking about everything and anything and spielberg spoke at his funeral i know the popular image of kubrick is a as a cold nihilist but dig beneath the surface and the truth is more nuanced but for the sake of argument lets take that quote at face value as its a point that many have argued ever since that schindlers list sanitizes and downplays the holocaust by presenting have a good night yes i understand this is yet another post about munich that seems to flair up every time the conflict flares up but i feel very little is discussed about how profoundly in depth the film is with its depiction of war and terror having watched it recently i was stunned by how morally ambiguous its themes are quick summary eric bana and a team of undercover israeli mossad agents embark on globe trotting mission to hunt down the perpetrators of the 1972 munich massacre surprisingly the film takes no sides at first the israelis are portrayed as the protagonists but theyre not exactly righteous heroes each step of their journey is a moral conundrum and as the bodies pile up they begin to question what theyre fighting for and more importantly if it will ever end the palestinians are depicted quite sincerely theyre not the typical mindless derka derka arab stereotype in most hollywood films theyre actual human beings wether its the sophisticated writer intellectual professor or determined young man you hear their arguments multiple times in fact they display fear anger sadness a point thats driven further by how raw violence in the film is depicted when people die its brutal cold and horrific blood sprays the walls corpses emit smoke bullets are so hot they ignite the bed sheets of the victim and when a man is shot he is in a state of shock dying slowly eyes gazed this is spielbergs most violent film it soon becomes apparent that the methods of the israelis are no different from the terrorists theyre hunting wether its dealing with the same criminal underbosses killing innocents and breaking international laws what started of as typical action adventure film soon becomes a cerebral psychological thriller as eric bana realises hes become a target as he fears for the safety of his family at the end of his mission hes not rewarded a medal or commendation for his effort rather a simple pat on the back furthermore he learns that the terrorists he killed have been immediately replaced so what was it all for a life of paranoia and the weight of all the lives hes taken in a never ending conflict politically munich is an incredibly balanced film ive always had issues with spielbergs simplistic sentimental approach to mature subjects personally not a fan of schindlers list or spr but thats a story for another day but munich offers a surprisingly nuanced and thought provoking take on what is continuously the worlds most contentious conflict citizen kane casablanca the godfather gone with the wind lawrence of arabia out kolchik and his raw and direct early work for his own band biting elbows one of few bands i religiously follow this entire cacophony of small scale but stylishly effective work both visual and aural has been an enormous influence and inspiration and coupled with the enthusiasm he displays in interviews hes one who i am dying to meet perhaps one day 4 alejandro g iñárritu birdman in the annals of world cinema both classic and modern id be happy to argue for iñárritus inclusion into that same circle one of few directors who strive to make no less than a masterpiece and for the most part hes succeeded with flying colors taking inspiration from classic directors while maintaining a modern and personal outlook iñárritu has crafted some of the most wildly ambitious inventive and intelligent movies in recent memory the revenant was the first movie of his i saw and if he had only made that film itd be enough to secure his legacy but he also had babel a devastating but empathetic work centering on human connection in the modern world he also has bardo a movie so all encompassing magical personal and overwhelming that it may be my favorite film 10 years down the line and he has birdman a film whose ideas are as fascinating as the movie is rewatchable thanks to its witty script indelible performances and of course the one shot camera technique that sucks you into this environment all making for the movie of his thats grown on me the strongest iñárritu is nothing short of a master and an artist frequently reaching for the stars and often landing among them 3 tetsuro araki attack on titan why so high and why a director of tv anime because he may just be my biggest influence growing up i saw 2d animation western and eastern as limited i saw it as unable to achieve the fluidity and versatility of 3d animation then i saw attack on titan and that assertion was utterly shattered for the first time i saw 2d animation live and breathe and hit so much harder than anything i had ever seen attack on titan was the anime to got me back into anime after leaving it for a while and i handily devoured anything that had tetsuro arakis name on it while guilty crown is the textbook definition of mediocre and highschool of the dead is too fanservicey to take seriously there was also death note one of few anime adaptations to surpass its source material thanks in large part to arakis direction theres kabaneri of the iron fortress which is deeply flawed but intensely paced and punchy theres even bubble a movie which im pretty sure im the only one that loves it what can i say it keeps the fluidity and polish of arakis previous work while taking it into a new direction one thats more romantic and emotional special props has to be given to arakis regular composer hiroyuki sawano whos about as perfect a partner to araki as john williams is to steven spielberg the epic orchestralelectronic music matching perfectly to arakis speedy and weighty animation out of all of these directors araki is the one whos made the most flawed work but hes also one of my biggest influences and inspirations and when i think of the ardor i have for his oeuvre it feels right to rank him this highly 2 hideaki anno ritual the biggest riser from my original top 10 its hard to imagine there was ever a time when i ranked anno at a mere number 6 ranking for some people stanley kubrick or david lynch were their introductions to nontraditional filmmaking to others it was jean luc godard or michelangelo antonioni for me it was hideaki anno watching neon genesis evangelion was a sea change in the way i saw filmmaking unearthing new possibilities that i could have never imagined and thats without going into the deeply personal aspects that affected me that was enough at the time to solidify anno as one of my favorite filmmakers but since then i saw love and pop a movie thats so inventively shot and edited that it would make breathless and citizen kane blush i saw cutie honey which showed a more lighthearted and bubbly side to him without sacrificing and ounce of talent or depth but most of all i saw ritual one of few movies to ever make a serious bid for being my single favorite film of all time and still the most personal movie to me that ive ever seen its practically a culmination of everything that makes anno who he is the preoccupation with escaping reality vs confronting it the unorthodox angles and compositions that are his trademarks the off kilter editing that keeps you on edge the striking and melancholic score that sticks with you and that inescapable feeling of loneliness the comfort that it brings and the pain of being ripped away from that loneliness but also ultimately the joy of it anno is simply too incredible a director movies that much for example my favorite and in my opinion the greatest movie of all time is schindlers list and one small part of that praise comes from how every shot is rich in detail whether through the harsh lighting the ingenious blockingstaging the distinct movement of the camera or usually all three at the same time schindlers list is a 3 hour and 15 minute movie to say nothing of the fact that its subject matter is difficult to go through if a 2 hour movie like vertigo takes several days to go through a shot at a time then a movie like schindlers list which is even longer and has potentially a lower average shot length would take at least a whole week or even two weeks if ever i did decide to examine it this way id have to properly pace myself and do it how ebert did it with a ark or 1917 and we can watch the whole movie in one go but of course these longer takes are often dense in detail and meaning and often hide their cuts so its no guarantee that picking a movie with 2012 list and vertigo a shot at a time how to read a movie movies id consider going through a shot at a time for reasons that are obvious i was interesting in revisiting munich this week i havent seen it since release and thought this would be a good opportunity i found it so compelling i ended up doing some long thoughts on it what struck me most is that of all spielbergs films there is none as conflicted and so full of conflict but there is also a boldness in it both as a film and a story that many of his historical films lack controversial on release for being an attempt at definitive commentary on the history of israelpalestine it is more clear now as a film about the concept of perpetual vengeance in george bushs america this is a movie by three jewish americans made at a time when america had seemingly lurched down its own road of unending conflict its ideas and concerns are surprisingly centred on america in 2005 more than israel in 1972 it is a wary tale that worries less about israels historical choices than the possibility that further conflict will be created and new cycles commenced the central premise exploring the cost of vengeance and justice on a people and individual people is delivered using a clever movie conceit transport the audience so wholly into a 70sstyle thriller that they almost forget what the movie is really about then when their guard is down hit them over the head with the context and it works this is one of spielbergs best directed and best shot films the 70s aesthetic grounds some of his most tense and nervewracking direction the camerawork is consistently sneaking shifting views and putting us at disease the use of mirrors and windows is in the annals of alltimegreat shot making imunichi should all be taught in film school and the net effect of this craft is to wrap us up in the momentum of the movie so much that when the consequences of geopolitical conflict interfere we are shaken just like the characters the films challenge is that it doesnt quite satisfy because the final act never finds way to tie up all the themes it toys with a significant part of the problem is the infamous sex montage which is relied on to provide the emotional climax but does not work any better now than on release the concept makes sense on paper but just cannot work in motion there is something simultaneously cheesy and in terrible taste about crosscutting a mans orgasm face with terrorists murdering olympic athletes even artists as skilled as spielberg kushner and roth cannot overcome the challenge the intensity of banas performance at that moment is also out of another movie more hilarious than moving it may be the biggest swing and miss of spielbergs career watching it now however there is a clear eyed boldness in what comes after that sequence and it redeems the film as a whole the movie ends with a falling out between banas avner and rushs ephraim then pans to the nyc skyline with the two towers in all their glory it neatly bundles ideas of jewishness eternal and disillusioned up with ideas of the american immigrant and the jewish diaspora but it also picks at a deep wound that resonates now more than ever the decision to use the two towers just 4 years after 911 and one year after bushs reelection largely through his touting of the iraq war was maybe too aggressive for audiences then it risked being too current and short sighted now with time it seems prescient avner is adopting his new life as a jewish american haunted by the unending war of his home country spielberg and his writers seem compelled to reflect on that new countrys own mistakes and their fear that a perpetual war was enveloping america in 2005 they would largely be right the war on terror continues to consume their new country and the haunting spectre of 911 and the madness it gave life to is very much of a kind with avners own madness and the films concern for israels approach to its own existence but it is a weird commentary where the films year of release and the global context at that time is in dialogue with the films subject matter the reaction from some loud israeli voices to the film condemning it for being antiisrael is understandable given where that mess of thematic dialogue not only does it question the impact of israels actions on the soul of its people but it also looks away from israel as it closes that a film about a seminal event in israels history ends up with its eye on america adds insult to injury for those who consider israels actions unquestionable but that is an unfair interpretation of the film because it assumes this is a film about israel when it is really one about global conflict more broadly especially in the 21st century three jewish americans made imunichi and so their concern is the soul of america and their contemporary world their connection to israel is not irrelevant but it is different they arent israeli by the end of the film it is clear that it is not about the soul of israel but about the soul of one man who emigrated to america when the price of his israeli identity became too high and those three jewish americans living in 2005 were in turn worried about what was happening to their nation and their souls it is undeniably messy and that explains why the film is so difficult and flawed its thematic complexity confusion is at odds with its remarkable execution as a hollywood thriller theres something rich in the mess however as spielberg kushner and roth struggle to process their own thoughts on one of the most complex geopolitical issues of our time they never manage to wrestle those thoughts into a precise thematic exploration but spielbergs talent as a filmmaker does perfect the other half so we end up with a monumental thriller wrapped in a challenging and confusing bow of world politics mess might be the best however given how fraught the very topic is if the themes were too neat too precise then the entire movie would work like clockwork it would be too smooth too entertaining and thereby lose its seriousness if there is any consistent flaw to spielbergs other big historical films be it ischindlers listi ilincolni or even ithe color purplei it is that they are almost too fun and too enjoyable for their subject matter imunichi is so effective as a thriller it would be too much if it also went down smoothly as political commentary better that we choke a little on its challenges balk at its sex montage ponder where its focus really lies issues that defy simple solutions for decades centuries are not well served by simplistic neat movies they deserve big swings and complex multifaceted thinking they deserve mess and ambition they deserve boldness they deserve movies like imunichi the other day someone commented on a 3 year old post i made titled my favorite directors and their best work it was a top 10 list of what else my favorite directors looking back i was fascinated by the list i made and i came away thinking that the more things change the more they stay the same for context my top 10 ranked from 101 was gareth edwards robert rodriguez james rolfe michael bay hideaki anno ilya naishuller alejandro g iñárritu tetsuro araki christopher nolan and steven spielberg as i mentioned in that post i was at the start of my film appreciation journey extremely green in classic world and nontraditional cinema in those 3 years ive expanded my horizons but ive only come to know how little i know i wryly and nostalgically look back at a time when i was so young and far less critical but again the more things change the more they stay the same all 10 directors i mentioned are still ones whose work i admire and many of them remain in the top 10 but the order is different my favorite film from them is different and of course there are many new directors whose filmographies ive come to deeply admire that top 10 has expanded to a top 20 and of course the order is constantly shifting as i go deeper into certain filmographies and discover new favorites so for fun id like to present a newish top 10 but first just like that last post a list of honorable mentions in no real order francis ford coppola peter watkins kiyoshi kurosawa stanley kubrick david lean michael bay gareth edwards robert rodriguez alfred hitchcock and the entire pixar company now onto the top 10 10 james rolfe cinemassacre 200 still on here from last time i see rolfe as a kind of gatekeeper to the top 10 a threshold of appreciation that other directors must surpass in order to make it to the top hes an unorthodox choice but hes an unorthodox filmmaker the godfather to the modern day youtuber especially in the gaming space but lets not forget that hes a filmmaker first one who refuses to let his lack of resources hold him back thus replacing budget with ingenuity hes very similar to robert rodriguez in that way his entire avgn series is a blast board james is insane dragon in my dreams is touching and cinemassacre 200 is still the most inspiring short documentary ive ever seen not in spite of its simplicity but because of it and all of this to say nothing of his movie reviews and discussions which bring to light interesting and striking movies often in the horror genre in a way thats as informative as it is entertaining hes like a modern day peter bogdanovich chronicling great movies and then learning from them in order to make his own if we were ranking directors by inspiration hed be number 1 9 christopher nolan memento no longer my second favorite director even though i rank 5 of his films as all time favorites even still i cant help but adore his style and stories complex and mainstream action packed and thoughtful breathlessly paced and epic in scope he doesnt always stick the landing but hes a genuine original and like him or not hes going to be the filmmaker to define the early 21st century of cinema oppenheimer is probably his magnum opus but my personal favorite will always be memento an ingenious neonoir that is a death spiral into the human psyche with one of the greatest the medium is the message structures in history 8 sidney lumet 12 angry men if theres any filmmaker who taught me the most about filmmaking it was sidney lumet through his excellent films yes but also through his book making movies the only book that should be required reading in film schools the reason that lumet was such a great teacher is simple hes one of the gifted few to actually get filmmaking in every aspect he utilized his talents and crews to create some of the most subtly affecting but densely packed movies in history not least of them being 12 angry men the ultimate example of doing and saying so much with so little but dont stop there soldier on with the stomach churning tension of failsafe which is almost as good look at the highly comedic and entertaining dog day afternoon and network which wear their social messages on their sleeve without shame or pretentiousness look at the overwhelming but still captivating long days journey into night a showcase of not just his deft usage of incredible actors but of subtly utilizing the film form to its absolute fullest containing maybe the greatest final shot of his career when all time great directors are discussed it is absolutely imperative that sidney lumet be included in the conversation 7 peter bogdanovich targets the latest director to enter the top 10 and while i always found him fascinating i never expected to see him as top 10 material the first and greatest film chronicler with his books like pieces of time who the devil made it and movie of the week being some of the most insightful discussions on the film form not least because they were written not just by a critic but by a director that he studied the great movies and then went on to make his own great movies is a testament to his understanding of movies not just his excellent big three of last picture show whats up doc and paper moon but also they all laughed mask noises off and the movie that made me dive headfirst into his work and one of the greatest unsung debutindependent american movies in history targets despite his chaotic and turbulent personal life and at times arrogant outlook bogdanovich was a one of a kind filmmaker who may not have been a role model personally but remains a role model professionally 6 mamoru hosoda wolf children the more personal you make your films the more universal they become i dont know if hosoda consciously thinks of this when he makes his movies but regardless hes created some of the most perfectly made joyously creative deeply intimate not just anime films but films in history hes far and away the greatest storyteller to fuse the fantastic and the realistic to create stories that resonate the longing of childhood in girl who leapt through time the complicated digital connections in belle the responsibility of siblings in mirai and the simple ecstasy and grief of changing and growing in wolf children which is my candidate for greatest anime film of all time hosoda and company are simply masters of their craft and for as incredible as their work is i sense that their greatest is still yet to come i for one will anxiously await their next masterpiece 5 ilya naishuller hardcore henry if there is a single director that id want to meet id be ilya naishuller if you havent noticed by now when i pick my favorite directors i consider everything that surrounds them their movies their style and if they have any other extraneous work outside of film like rolfes reviews and bogdanovichs books same with lumet naishuller is no different he may only have two films to his name but one of those is my all time favorite action movie and one of my 5 favorites hardcore henry outside of movies he has a litany of excellent music videos from other first person extravaganzas like false alarm and bad motherfucker to transgressive and creative narratives in his work for the band leningrad check out kolchik and his raw and direct early work for his own band biting elbows one of few bands i religiously follow this entire cacophony of small scale but stylishly effective work both visual and aural has been an enormous influence and inspiration and coupled with the enthusiasm he displays in interviews hes one who i am dying to meet perhaps one day 4 alejandro g iñárritu birdman in the annals of world cinema both classic and modern id be happy to argue for iñárritus inclusion into that same circle one of few directors who strive to make no less than a masterpiece and for the most part hes succeeded with flying colors taking inspiration from classic directors while maintaining a modern and personal outlook iñárritu has crafted some of the most wildly ambitious inventive and intelligent movies in recent memory the revenant was the first movie of his i saw and if he had only made that film itd be enough to secure his legacy but he also had babel a devastating but empathetic work centering on human connection in the modern world he also has bardo a movie so all encompassing magical personal and overwhelming that it may be my favorite film 10 years down the line and he has birdman a film whose ideas are as fascinating as the movie is rewatchable thanks to its witty script indelible performances and of course the one shot camera technique that sucks you into this environment all making for the movie of his thats grown on me the strongest iñárritu is nothing short of a master and an artist frequently reaching for hope that it is because it is equal parts sexy hilarious and tragic 10 saturday night directed by jason reitman being up front i am a huge snl stan and will always admire those that have made it onto the show what not many know about though is just how much of a maverick this show and lorne were the executives did not give this show much of a chance along with some of lornes actors going awol hours before the show was supposed to air and a set that literally ignited hours before the first show how lorne actually got this program off the ground is just as amazing as it is for him to keep it running for 50 years and this film does an admirable job showing the chaos and love that is behind this cultural staple in tv history 9 memoir of a snail directed by adam elliot my favorite animated film this year adam directed another film a while back called max mary that was also very well done memoir of a snail pretty much adds elements that were lacking in the previous film and enhances it to help become an even more personal experience about a melancholic woman who hoards snails to as a way to shelter herself from all of the stressors that have occurred in her life its tragic and hopeful and makes you hope for more adult animated films 8 civil war directed by alex garland the title speaks for itself doesnt it it is a film taking place through the lens of war photographers these people help push alexs bigger story about what it might look like if it all goes to hell in this country and he doesnt make it easy this movie reminded me a lot of contagion in how it tried to ground itself to make it feel like this can happen the only difference between contagion and civil war is that this film actually moved me where i felt shocked and depressed after it is a very bleak outlook on our future but one that i cant help but respect just for even attempting to make an uncomfortable movie like civil war 7 a real pain directed by jesse eisenberg another lived experience film that hits deeply to me due to my own cultural roots as a jewish person i question my existence constantly and this film pretty much encapsulates that experience for me as a millennial who has a direct lineage to family members who passed away our survived from the holocaust the love we have for others run deep and it can be exhausting but we still love them despite what they do and its what i love and appreciate about this film the most 6 alien romulus directed by fede alvarez ive waited too long to finally watch a proper alien movie on a movie screen and i finally got it with this addition by fede this film pretty much takes the genre back to its roots about just why this franchise is so terrifying to begin with the movie intentionally moves at a slow pace at first but once the characters go inside that incubator chamber 01000 in an instant and it doesnt let up until it ends this is exactly what fans of the alien franchise had been hoping for and im glad i can finally say i saw a legitimately terrifying alien movie on the big screen in my life 5 dune part ii directed by denis villeneuve if you thought part i of this epic saga on arrakis was wild the 2nd part pretty much adds onto everything with strong performances by all who were involved but especially javier bardem who channels his inner monty python to deliver a hilarious but also very realistic take on a devout follower who believes that timothee is the mahdi and the truth is by the end of this movie i was ready to crown him the mahdi myself this was an epic continuation of a saga that has one more battle to be prepared for 4 wicked part i directed by jon chu i love broadway i find it a fantastic medium where craft music acting and dancing come to life all in an instant translating a broadway show to become a movie is not easy but jon and his crew knocked it out of the part with the first rendition of wicked i found myself loving a lot of parts to this movie from the singing to the feeling of being transported back into oz for the first time since watching the original to arianas rendition of popular to defying gravity to the fact that 2 hours and 40 minutes did not feel that long to the fact that the movie ended like it was the intermission and not the true end to the film jon and his team seemed to spare no expense when it came to making this musical feel like were watching the broadway play at a massive discount and i appreciate them for that because broadway plays are expensive they all delivered a stunning film that feels every bit as magical as the first time we all stepped into oz what is great for them now is they can ride the momentum of the first film into part ii next year without doing anything because they already finished the shoot cant deny it is a brilliant marketing strategy that will probably make massive money in the long run i know im going to be there opening night to see the finale 3 kill directed by nikhil nagesh bhat as much as i love musicals i love one man armys even more and this genuinely could be the scariest one man army i have ever come across i first learned about this film from one of those trailers that are packed with reviews that say you have to see this movie since i had nothing better to do on july 4th i figured why not and im very glad i did that because amrit rathod is the scariest dude walking the planet i am fully convinced he can take on jason bourne and john wick and come out on top after watching this insanity make no mistake amrit is a different animal compared to the others he lives by a code and once its broken you better start preparing your last rites cause he will break you down until youre absolutely nothing and then he will kill you i honestly think this movie couldve possibly pushed the genre even further because of some of the there are simply kills that i have not seen before on the big screen and this movie was not shy at all in delivering them 2 the zone of interest directed by jonathan glazer probably the most educational film about the holocaust that has ever been made or aside from since schindlers list what i love about this movie is that unlike schindler there is nothing good about this film it is awful and you come to realize just how banal we truly are as a species once its done i found myself growing more detested as the film continued and that is its point at one time this was not a movie this was a real worldchanging event that occurred where people were sent to concentration camps to suffer and die and all of us as a species grew desensitized to the point that we did not feel any remorse for these fellow humans and truth be told i still dont all the while the scum were standing less than 20 feet away pretending that they were not committing the atrocities that they were this is the inverse of one life where instead of educating us on the good that humans are capable of it shows us just how evil we really are and that is admittedly a much bigger reality than the miracles and good people that do occur during these awful times in order to possibly prevent it in the future we need to still educate today and this film is as educational as it gets 1 american fiction directed by cord jefferson its unfortunate this movie came out in the completely packed year that was 2023 because this is easily one of my favorite american experience movies i remember and i think its because of how mundane it is that i love it so much the characters are going through their own sets of trauma that would be very similar to ours and also have a dynamic that feels very natural to the relationships we have with our loved ones jeffery wright and sterling k brown also give fantastic performances that are equally hilarious endearing and thoughtprovoking as well what is so fantastic too is how much they make the dialogue feel natural it really feels like youre living the life with this writer as he uncovers the hypocrisy and racial dynamics in an incredibly niche aspect of society the way jefferson makes it all thread perfectly was so much fun my favorite part though that cements this as my favorite has to be the ending its kind of abrupt but also in this very well done way that reminds us that just like the main characters in this movie life will continue onwards and its what we do with it that is really going to matter the most rereleases i watched in addition to the 71 films i watched i also saw two rereleases coraline and interstellar to total out to 73 movies i went to see conclusion i saw a lot more films than i did last year which is great because it hopefully shows to me that the industry itself is at least churning out regular features again if you wouldve told me this back in 2020 theres no way id believe you while the recovery isnt there yet and there are genuine critiques on a lack of originality its nice to know how strong this year was when it comes to regular releases so with that i hope you enjoyed warning ill start off by saying that i found zone of interest to be extremely effective the phrase show dont tell comes up often in regards to the efficacy of exposition zone of interest takes this to an extreme something i would call imply dont tell the banality of evil is depicted better by not showing the evils of nazism only implying them largely through reference inference and implication like others i was a little confused at first by the jarring ending of zone of interest involving a cut to the modernday auschwitz museumspecifically the army of employeesvolunteers cleaning and dusting the exhibits before opening is it merely a flash forward in time to give a greater sense of the horrors we have only heard off screen muffled and distant what is with the cleaning ladies this ending became much clearer in intent in my mind after finishing the miniseries masters of the air mota follows in the footsteps of band of brothers and the pacific to complete a trip of wwii miniseries produced by steven spielberg and tom hanks while i largely enjoyed mota as i did the previous two series i was left with a sour taste in my mouth after finishing it largely because of the choices made in the last episode while the series avoided narratives surrounding the holocaust a plot involving the lone jewish character crashlanding behind russian lines being rescued and stumbling into a liberated death camps is egregiously shoehorned into the final episode and upon watching the outofnowhere forced scene of goldie in shock while the camera pans over piles upon piles of charred corpse pouring out of ovens i realized what jonathan glazer intended by the ending of zone of interest remember how i said that my family history has influenced how i see holocaust media well my father shared an anecdote with me years ago that i still think on while talking about schindlers list and the impact it had on culture when it came out he said more or less the following i saw schindlers list once it was a well made movie a few clients who came into my office and knew i was jewish mentioned seeing it to me because it was the first time many gentiles had been exposed to a story like that but i dont feel a need to ever watch it again we know those stories because we grew up with them from our families from our friends and from our communities spielberg didnt make that film for us he made it for the gentiles ive thought plenty on that and i feel more or less the same way i find myself not viewing much holocaust media because it is simply not providing anything new or helpful in how we look back on the holocaust i generally like spielberg but his take on nazism largely starts and ends with nazis bad while i will admit attitudes and education regarding the holocaust are at an alltime low and perhaps we do need reminders of that from time to time however when it comes to understanding how the nazis were capable of perpetrating their evils we are sorely lacking at least we were until zone of interest i believe that in addition to largely being about the banality of evil and how someone could discard their baseline humanity i think that glazer is taking a direct shot at spielberg and his technique of blasting his audience in the chest from point blank with his emotional shotgun of corpses and ovens ironically by showing so much spielberg is sanitizing our narratives of the holocaust and preventing dialogue that delves deeper into the how and why of nazism when glazer shows the display of piles of shoes from murdered victims he is showing us a deliberate arrangement much the same as spielberg deliberately arranges his images for maximum emotional impact but both are artificial they are constructs made after the fact to create a visceral response in the viewer by showing the cleaning ladies wiping the glass in front of the shoe display case glazer is saying that we have sanitized our images of the holocaust by only showing the most graphic images that demand emotional responses but glossing over possible dialogue going deeper on the nature of nazism fascism and evil in general i think this ending is also similar to the ending of killers of the flower moon it is an acknowledgement that regardless of the content a film is still largely a commercial product made to be viewed by an audience not a replacement for history so how do we effectively create films that address the nittygritty details of how fascism can rise and take hold even in a population that largely believes that something like that could never happen here lucky for us that movie already exists and its called starship troopers paul verhoevens starship troopersis known for its strange place in culture and its journey from misunderstood action flop to revered satirical masterpiece but i believe it is the single best most important depiction of the specific details that make a society fascist first i believe its important to define fascism else we fall into the spielberg trap of ending our thought at nazis bad imho umberto ecos essay on urfascism is the best set of characteristics of a fascist society regardless of flavour 1 the cult of tradition characterized by cultural syncretism even at the risk of internal contradiction when all truth has already been revealed by tradition no new learning can occur only further interpretation and refinement 2 the rejection of modernism which views the rationalistic development of western culture since the enlightenment as a descent into depravity eco distinguishes this from a rejection of superficial technological advancement as many fascist regimes cite their industrial potency as proof of the vitality of their system 3 the cult of action for actions sake which dictates that action is of value in itself and should be taken without intellectual reflection this says eco is connected with antiintellectualism and irrationalism and often manifests in attacks on modern culture and science 4 disagreement is treason fascism devalues intellectual discourse and critical reasoning as barriers to action as well as out of fear that such analysis will expose the contradictions embodied in a syncretistic faith 5 fear of difference which fascism seeks to exploit and exacerbate often in the form of racism or an appeal against foreigners and immigrants 6 appeal to a frustrated middle class fearing economic pressure from the demands and aspirations of lower social groups 7 obsession with a plot and the hypingup of an enemy threat this often combines an appeal to xenophobia with a fear of disloyalty and sabotage from marginalized groups living within the society eco also cites pat robertsons book the new world order as a prominent example of a plot obsession 8 fascist societies rhetorically cast their enemies as at the same time too strong and too weak on the one hand fascists play up the power of certain disfavored elites to encourage in their followers a sense of grievance and humiliation on the other hand fascist leaders point to the decadence of those elites as proof of their ultimate feebleness in the face of an overwhelming popular will 9 pacifism is trafficking with the enemy because life is permanent warfare there must always be an enemy to fight both fascist germany under hitler and italy under mussolini worked first to organize and clean up their respective countries and then build the war machines that they later intended to and did use despite germany being under restrictions of the versailles treaty to not build a military force this principle leads to a fundamental contradiction within fascism the incompatibility of ultimate triumph with perpetual war 10 contempt for the weak which is uncomfortably married to a chauvinistic popular elitism in which every member of society is superior to outsiders by virtue of belonging to the ingroup eco sees in these attitudes the root of a deep tension in the fundamentally hierarchical structure of fascist polities as they encourage leaders to despise their underlings up to the ultimate leader who holds the whole country in contempt for having allowed him to overtake it by force 11 everybody is educated to become a hero which leads to the embrace of a cult of death as eco observes the urfascist hero is impatient to die in his impatience he more frequently sends other people to death 12machismo which sublimates the difficult work of permanent war and heroism into the sexual sphere fascists thus hold both disdain for women and intolerance and condemnation of nonstandard sexual habits from chastity to homosexuality 13 selective populism the people conceived monolithically have a common will distinct from and superior to the viewpoint of any individual as no mass of people can ever be truly unanimous the leader holds himself out as the interpreter of the popular will though truly he alone dictates it fascists use this concept to delegitimize democratic institutions they accuse of no longer representing the voice of the people 14 newspeak fascism employs and promotes an impoverished vocabulary in order to limit critical reasoning starship troopers filmmaker and later on seems to dismiss its merits entirely saying i see a society plunged into poverty by gangsterism masquerading as poetry its prettiness is a tactic i just think it offers a broad distortion of a misunderstood regime to get into the nitty gritty of cubas politics and history and lords own connection to both is a quagmire that i have neither the information nor will to wade into so id like to focus primarily on film as a form of propaganda and the way audiences receive said propaganda personally i think lord is virtue signaling here and im not a fan of him as a person but he raises an interesting point that ive been thinking about for a long time propaganda and cinema have been intertwined since the film camera was invented but i always noticed that certain kinds of propaganda movies get treated differently than others casablanca is the kind of movie that may as well be propaganda it was even rushed into release to cash in on the us invasion of italy but its such a miracle of a movie that over 80 years later were still watching it because casablanca never pretends to be a realistic telling of a true story and is more about the characters than anything else it becomes a kind of fairy tale whos truths resonate today the same way something like cinderella does also if youre an american like i am then naturally were liable to swallow its ideals more easily and since to us ww2 is the good war we can hold it up without feeling ashamed of it but then theres the films of leni riefenstahl and sergei eisenstein with riefenstahl being a member of the nazi party and explicitly making her films to further their ideology its difficult to praise her movies no matter how revolutionary they are olympia is the best of her work and also the one least tainted by nazism but its stain remains and is hard to ignore and then theres eisenstein explicitly tied into the communist party of the ussr and making movies to further their ideology however hes not given the same amount of scrutiny despite the soviet union committing atrocities of a similar nature to the nazis i see there being multiple reasons for this one is that despite communism being well communism the violence that tends to arise from it is directed towards a class of people the socalled bourgeoise and its aftermath as an unfortunate and unforeseen byproduct im not saying that this is right or wrong only how it tends to be perceived by its adherents nazism directs its violence towards a specific race of people namely the jews as well as anyone who isnt of aryan descent this kind of ideology causes deep discomfort in the west due to our history with race so were more resistant to the nazi backed films of riefenstahl while being wary but ultimately open to the soviet backed films of eisenstein the us also has a complicated relationship with the rich with some idolizing them some despising them two while the waters are muddy on this topic it seems clear that riefenstahl was far more buddy buddy with hitler than eisenstein was with stalin riefenstahl held a personal friendly relationship with hitler while eisenstein fell in and out of favor with stalin throughout his life this allows us to paint the picture of riefenstahl as a collaborator of the nazi regime while eisenstein can be painted as an artist trying to create his works in spite of instead of instead of formal innovations are so staggering and ahead of its time that to dismiss out of hand would be silly at the same time it is true that its ultimate purpose is that of propaganda a purpose that it ironically failed at since cubans saw it as stereotypical and russians saw it as not politically revolutionary enough perhaps this is another reason that it garners the attention it does in the west driving miss daisy 49th green book 50th crash title schindlers list the game genre stealth adventure historical platform pc ps5 xbox series x description schindlers list the game is a stealthadventure game that puts you in the role of oskar schindler a german industrialist who saved the lives of over a thousand jews during the holocaust the game follows the events of the movie as you use your influence cunning and resources to bribe persuade and deceive the nazis while secretly rescuing and protecting your jewish workers the game features realistic graphics voice acting and historical accuracy as well as multiple choices and outcomes that affect the story and the fate of your workers the game also includes a moral system that tracks your actions and decisions and a list system that shows you the names and faces of the people you have saved or lost gameplay the game is divided into several chapters each corresponding to a different period and location of the movie in each chapter you have to complete various objectives such as acquiring a factory hiring workers obtaining materials producing goods negotiating with the nazis and smuggling your workers to safety you can explore the environments interact with characters and use items and money to achieve your goals however you also have to avoid suspicion and detection from the nazis who will try to stop you and harm your workers you can use stealth distraction persuasion or bribery to evade or deal with them but you also have to face the consequences of your actions the game also features several cinematic sequences and quicktime events that recreate the most memorable scenes from the movie is over for today ordinary people was eliminated comeback tomorrow morning at 10 for the new thread the game continues different people who were able to find a common language in a critical situation in june 1975 spielberg first showed the world his talent how he knows how to glue the viewer to the screen how naturalistically he shows the routine of his characters how he works with children how carefully he treats details and of course how coolly he frames the frame surprisingly in the 21st century jaws has lost neither its charm nor its dynamics nor its horror nor its stature the opening scene with the painful death of a girl is still shocking the severed head of a fisherman still frightens those who are watching the film for the first time hunter quints monologue about the cruiser indianapolis is still touching the film is based on the novel jaws by peter benchley 1974 the story of its creation gives the film a special flair it became the first american film to be shot in the open ocean the director still claims that refusing to shoot in a studio pool was the most naive and stupid decision in his life the shark was constantly breaking down sudden changes in the weather slowed down the already protracted filming yachts and boats kept appearing in the background they had to wait until they disappeared from view in addition the entire film crew got seasick and the main characters ship almost sank at one point but it was worth it jaws changed the landscape of the film business forever steven shot the first blockbuster in history essentially becoming the father of an entire film trend partly thanks to his work all the major hollywood franchises that we know today were born the first film in the history of distribution that managed to conquer the coveted 100 million mark there was more to come the shark saga turned out to be only the first cult series launched by spielberg in the 80s he created indiana jones in the 90s jurassic park the director has always had a keen sense of the demands of the general public and satisfied them with undisguised perfectionism it is important to give him credit steven did not entrench himself strictly in an entertaining onetrack genre on the contrary the director was always in search of experiments and complex themes so 18 years later he released the monumental schindlers list a little later saving private ryan then minority report his career is that very hollywood fairy tale that became reality and it began 50 years ago when he made one of the pearls of the new hollywood on par with the godfather taxi driver the exorcist when he together with his outstanding colleagues showed that cinema can be like this the tba i hope its ok to post this here im not trying to be inflammatory as i dont really discuss the actual plot of the movie or the actors i finally watched schindlers list and i just need to get this off my chest and have no one else to talk to about how i feel i tend to stay away from movies that depict historical violence i can sit through documentaries and educational content that contains nsfl footage and testimonies i guess i can chalk it up to me just being sensitive and easily triggered the movie itself was an absolute masterpiece that being said i went through all three hours completely unphased i didnt cry when i saw children being killed en mass i didnt gasp when i saw the piles of dead bodies at no point did i look away because the horror was too much to bear im numb and desensitized not just because of whats happening in gaza the congo sudan but because ive seen how the current barbarity is a mere drop in the bucket compared to the collective atrocities we as a species inflicted on ourselves for centuries year after year decade after decade millennia after millennia the weaponization of fear fuels hate and violence to serve the greed and desires of people with power over others to me its everything everywhere all at once if that makes any sense i will say that although i was unphased during the movie i couldnt sleep a wink afterwards my mind couldnt rest thinking of all the parallels between the holocaust the history of indigenous people who were colonized or annihilated state sanctioned violence against its own citizens and the reality of the daily unfathomable torment that people are experiencing today i think part of what kept me up is the understanding that this unbridled horror is avoidable but the people with the power to orchestrate these atrocities have decided its in their best interest to have the masses believe there iswas no way to prevent the carnage my ultimate takeaway from the film is that those who can see beyond the shadows and lies of their culture will never be understood let alone believed by the masses plato one day when the truth comes to light everyone will be against insert whatever massmanufactured atrocity here by then it will have been too late to save ourselves from the consequences of our collective inaction i cannot begin to conceptualize the physical and psychological pain that the dead victims who lived through the previous and present tortuous times experienced not even to speak of the survivors where does all of their pain go which is the best film or films you have ever seen that profoundly captures and reveals the evilness in humanity i could give some examples but it would go on forever from the most obvious ones schindlers list come and see salo a clockwork orange 12 years a slave killers of the flower moon the pianist irreversible funny games do you agree what are your choices we all know the big tearjerker movies up and schindlers list and all accompanying pixarholocaust movies they tell stories that are highly charged with emotion and thus lend themselves to making the audience cry but then there are movies where crying is seemingly not the intention and yet you cant help but feel your eyes well up in tears the godfather part 2 is called a lot of things the best sequel ever made one of the best movies of the 70s the great american movie but a tearjerker from the outset it doesnt really look it and yet if you find yourself sucked into michael corleones inner turmoil as i was it becomes surprisingly easy to become emotional the further along you go in especially whenmichael gives the implicit order to kill fredo and before he meets his inevitable fate his attempts to connect with michaels sonill admit i wept i wept pretty hard and i did not expect that reaction from the godfather 2 what movie was like that for you weve all seen our tear jerker movies schindlers list sophies choice requiem for a dream etc or movies with iconic sad you cry right well i watched it with my wife for the first time the other night for some bizarre reason we merged our final destination marathon with our bill and ted marathon wed watch a couple final destinations then a bill and ted per night over a few nights the contrast was surreal death goes from killing people with rube goldberg level complexity in final destination to chilling and playing realize its their daughters that write the song that unites the world and realty not them and they kind of pass the torch to them and offer their support and i just lost it my dad committed suicide all these things were happening i really felt the weight of the decisions the darkness of amon goth just hit differently spielberg was right about the eyes it was all about the flickers and subtle shifts in the eyes to this sexual darkness to sinisterness to an almost immature human who admired yet was cautious of oskar oskar being a womaniser and yet so caring for the jews at his own expense i really felt his morals and care and it stood out to me when the soldiers were laughing at him at spraying the water hose on the jews in the train and instead of reacting and being offended he plays it off like a chess game using their arrogance to his advantage i mourned the ghetto ethnic cleansing the children just broke my heart especially the toilet scene and the girl with the red coat gosh that hits hard that adults could do that izthaks subtleness also with the eyes and the way he spoke i actually didnt notice the tear he shed when they shared the drink the first time i saw the film the cinematography definitely stood out to me even amongst all the horrors there were shots that were incredibly well done with beautiful hair lights and lighting of the faces the conversation between amon and oskar on the balcony where oskar is talking about power was one of the best lit scenes finally the ash scene and the open pit burning scenes shocked me that one reminded me most of dachau because i saw the ovens there next was the pianist very different and what stood out to me was the gradual descent into destruction i noticed the saturation change more this time and also the justifications in their minds first nearby people then neighbours then themselves yet also psychologically how the constant adaptation was occurring what i noticed this time was how personal it felt it really felt that roman yes i know his history really injected the interactions that would have happened during that time being played with being shot in the head for simply asking a question and then the bodies justbeing there there was this sense of this was a normal day for them the starving man who steals the soup and then eats it off the floor really hit the desperation for me that communicated the starvation so strongly the change in władysławs eyes is again what came through and when hes dishevelled when the german captain finds him it dawned upon me how much he had diminished and i legit thought he was going to collapse at any time from weakness that was actually when i noticed reduction of jews on screen throughout the movie at first they were everywhere then over the movie there is justless and less of them then the sudden shift at the end when the captain is in the soviet camp and władysław is playing piano again all cleaned up with the warm lighting and interiors and a room full of people i actually wasnt prepared for that shift in tone again i hadnt seen the pianist probably since my teenage years so now after both films i feel this movement of jurassic park to be one of the strangest and most disturbing films i have ever seen as much as i enjoy rewatching the film i cant help but be reminded of an eerie conclusion i reached long ago although never pieced together until recently i realize that the film is based on a michael crichton novel and that he actually wrote a lot of the screenplay however perhaps spielbergs main interest in retelling the story was to use it as an allegory for the holocaust jurassic park was spielbergs way of portraying the holocaust in a subtle yet horrific way that he could not do in schindlers list there a lot parallels which i struggle to dismiss for example the poster of the film uses red and black for the menacing jurassic park logo the films premise involves a group of unsuspecting people visiting a park utterly oblivious to the danger that hides behind the stone walls and electrified fences enclosing them the jurassic park vehicles rattle along a rail line just like a train cart the power fails and the most horrifying scenario imaginable becomes a reality for these guests being shepherded around the park there are several scenes in both of spielbergs films that resemble each other such as those shots in front of the jurassic park and auschwitz main gates there is that kitchen scene where the two children hide in in compartments while being chased by the raptors there are of course many violent scenes of death and torture carried out by the dinosaurs as well there are the constant references to extinction you have the character of dr hammond who is overtly concerned with his own financial prospects rather than the safety of others sort of similar to oskar schindler they both eventually realizing that saving people that they have unintentionally endangered must come before all else there is also the line about how life always finds away possibly echoing how antisemitism always finds a way the film ends with a helicopter arriving to rescue the remaining survivors of the park the final message being that never again should dinosaurs rear their monstrous heads and never again should any person be subjected to such inhumane terror 3 hours runtime in a
